projects.title: Hydro-Risk Price Forecasting Model
projects.tagline: Regime-sensitive modelling of NZ wholesale electricity price risk, showing tail risk roughly doubles once hydro storage drops into the bottom decile.
projects.org: Massey University — Master of Analytics Capstone (Paper 115.801)

businessProblem.context: New Zealand's wholesale electricity price is heavily exposed to hydro storage conditions, but that exposure isn't linear — a small change in storage can mean a very different price-risk environment depending on where storage sits relative to historical norms. Wellington Electricity needed a way to see that regime shift coming rather than reacting to it after prices had already spiked.
goals: Build a regime-sensitive model linking hydro storage, tributary inflows, HVDC transfer flows, spill volumes, and climate indicators to wholesale price risk.
Quantify how tail risk (VaR/CVaR) changes specifically once storage falls into a low-storage regime, rather than assuming a constant relationship.
Turn the regime signal into an early-warning indicator that procurement and risk teams can act on ahead of price moves.
painPoints: Monthly storage, inflow, spill, HVDC and climate data lived across nine separate source folders with inconsistent formats, requiring a full standardisation and validation pass before any modelling could start.
A single linear regression across all storage levels masked what was actually happening — the real risk was concentrated in a specific low-storage regime, not spread evenly across the range.
HVDC transfer data limited the reliable estimation window to 1996-2019 (241 months), which had to be handled carefully rather than just running the model on all 279 available months of price data.

approach.methodology: Standardised and merged nine separate data sources into a single monthly panel (1996-2024, 279 observations), then classified each month into a storage regime using percentile thresholds — with the low-storage regime defined as national active storage at or below the 10th percentile (~34% of active capacity). Estimated OLS regression models with HC3 robust standard errors, moving from a simple storage-only model (R² = 0.167) to a multi-driver model adding HVDC flow, spill, lagged inflow anomaly and lagged price (R² = 0.704), then validated the storage threshold non-parametrically with a spline basis. Computed Value-at-Risk and Conditional VaR at the 95th and 99th percentiles separately for the normal and low-storage regimes to quantify tail-risk amplification, and tested a short-horizon early-warning specification to flag regime entry ahead of time.
dataSources: National hydro storage levels (active storage, % of capacity)

insights: Mean wholesale price under low-storage conditions was $300.96/MWh, versus $173.58/MWh in normal conditions and $140.97/MWh in high-storage conditions — the low-storage regime alone explains most of the price variation the model captures.
In dry-year proxy months, mean price was $438.16/MWh versus $165.21/MWh in normal months — more than double, and a materially bigger gap than the broader low-storage regime shows on its own.

Tail risk moves more than the mean does: 95th-percentile VaR jumps from $717.97/MWh (normal) to $2,026.16/MWh (low storage), and CVaR95 rises from $1,511.22 to $2,118.51 — roughly a 40% increase in expected loss beyond the 95th percentile once storage crosses the low-storage threshold.

The storage effect is non-linear: a simple storage-only regression explains little (R² = 0.167), but adding an explicit low-storage interaction term (coefficient -20.656, p < 0.001) and lagged drivers (HVDC flow, spill, inflow anomaly, lagged price) lifts explanatory power to R² = 0.704, with lagged price the strongest single driver — evidence that price shocks persist rather than resetting each month.

visualizations.title: National Hydro Storage, 1996-2024
visualizations.description: Monthly active hydro storage over the full sample period, showing the recurring dry-year troughs that drive the low-storage regime.

visualizations.title: Price Distribution by Storage Regime
visualizations.description: Wholesale price spread is both higher and far more volatile once storage drops into the bottom decile (LowStorage_10 = 1), 1996-2019.

visualizations.title: Price Distribution: Normal vs Low Storage (Tail Focus)
visualizations.description: Overlaying the two regimes' price histograms shows the low-storage distribution carries materially more mass in the extreme tail.

visualizations.title: Price Under Hydro Stress Scenarios
visualizations.description: Normal, dry (P10) and severe-dry (P05) storage scenarios compared — the severe-dry case shows a dramatically wider price range.

recommendations: Use the ~34%-of-capacity storage threshold as a hedge-escalation trigger — a concrete, monitorable line rather than a qualitative 'storage is getting low' judgement call.

Size hedging and procurement buffers off CVaR95 by regime, not off the unconditional average price — the ~40% jump in expected tail loss under low storage is the number that actually matters for exposure planning.

Because lagged price is the strongest driver in the multi-driver model, treat a live price spike as informative about near-term persistence, not just a one-off data point, when deciding whether to escalate hedging.

Extend the early-warning specification (built on a 3-month-ahead dry-regime flag) into a monitoring routine that flags regime entry before storage fully crosses the threshold, giving procurement a lead-time advantage rather than a reactive one.
projects.roi: Quantified that expected tail-loss exposure (CVaR95) rises roughly 40% once hydro storage falls into the bottom decile, and identified a concrete, monitorable storage threshold (~34% of active capacity) that can anchor a hedge-escalation trigger rather than relying on qualitative judgement.

codeSnippets.title: Storage Regime Classification
codeSnippets.code:
STORAGE_COL = "Storage_active_mm"
PRICE_COL = "Price_mean"

# Create storage regimes using quantiles
q_low = df[STORAGE_COL].quantile(0.20)
q_high = df[STORAGE_COL].quantile(0.80)

def storage_regime(x):
    if x <= q_low:
        return "Low storage"
    elif x >= q_high:
        return "High storage"
    else:
        return "Normal storage"

df["Storage_Regime"] = df[STORAGE_COL].apply(storage_regime)

# Tighter low-storage flag used for the headline regime (bottom decile)
q10 = reg_df["Storage_pct"].quantile(0.10)
reg_df["LowStorage_10"] = (reg_df["Storage_pct"] <= q10).astype(int)

codeSnippets.title: Tail-Risk: VaR & CVaR by Regime
codeSnippets.code:
def var_cvar(x, q=0.95):
    x = pd.Series(x).dropna().values
    v = np.percentile(x, 100*q)
    c = x[x >= v].mean() if (x >= v).any() else np.nan
    return v, c

rows = []
for label, subset in [("Normal", tail_df[tail_df["LowStorage_10"]==0]),
                      ("LowStorage_10", tail_df[tail_df["LowStorage_10"]==1])]:
    v95, c95 = var_cvar(subset["Price_mean"], 0.95)
    v99, c99 = var_cvar(subset["Price_mean"], 0.99)
    rows.append({
        "Regime": label,
        "n": len(subset),
        "VaR95": v95,
        "CVaR95": c95,
        "VaR99": v99,
        "CVaR99": c99
    })

tail_risk_by_regime = pd.DataFrame(rows)

projects.title: Credit Risk Segmentation & Customer Clustering
projects.tagline: Segmenting 30,000+ credit users into behavioural risk groups to enable risk-based pricing.
projects.org: Massey University — Multivariate Analysis for Big Data

businessProblem.context: Lenders benefit from grouping customers by behavioural risk rather than relying on a single blended risk score, so pricing can reflect actual risk profile.
goals: Segment a large credit user base into distinct behavioural risk groups.
Validate the segmentation with a classification approach.
Enable risk-based pricing from the resulting groups.
painPoints: High-dimensional customer data is hard to segment meaningfully without dimensionality reduction.
Segmentation needs to be validated, not just visually plausible.

approach.methodology: Applied PCA and clustering to segment 30,000+ credit users into behavioural risk groups, then validated the segmentation with discriminant analysis.

insights: Discriminant analysis validated the behavioural clusters with 94% classification accuracy.
Distinct behavioural risk groups emerged that a single blended score would not surface.
recommendations: Use the validated segments as the basis for risk-based pricing tiers.
Re-run segmentation periodically as behavioural patterns shift.
projects.roi: 94% classification accuracy validating the behavioural segments, enabling risk-based pricing by group.

projects.title: Financial Hardship Modelling
projects.tagline: Quantifying how income, gambling harm, and financial literacy interact to drive financial hardship.

businessProblem.context: Policy decisions around financial hardship support are stronger when they're based on how income, gambling harm, and financial literacy interact, rather than treating each factor in isolation.
goals: Model the interactions between income, gambling harm, and financial literacy.
Quantify their combined impact on financial hardship.
Support policy recommendations with statistically robust findings.
painPoints: These factors are correlated with each other, so isolating their individual contribution requires careful model diagnostics.
Findings needed to hold up to robustness checks to be usable for policy.

approach.methodology: Modelled interactions between income, gambling harm, and financial literacy using robust OLS regression, applying diagnostics to check the stability of the findings before drawing policy conclusions.
dataSources: Survey data on income, gambling harm, and financial literacy

insights: Income, gambling harm, and financial literacy interact rather than acting independently on hardship outcomes.
Robust regression diagnostics confirmed the interaction effects held up beyond a simple linear read of the data.
recommendations: Target policy support at the specific combination of risk factors identified, not each factor independently.
Use the diagnostic-checked model as a baseline for further hardship research.
projects.roi: Produced statistically robust findings on hardship drivers to support policy recommendations.

projects.title: KPI & Governance Dashboard Reporting
projects.tagline: Consolidating fragmented multi-source data into standardised KPI dashboards across 80+ global agencies.

businessProblem.context: KPI, SLA, billing, and utilisation data for 7 Omnicom networks and 80+ global agencies was spread across separate systems (Replicon, Hive, Jira, Adobe Workfront), making reporting to senior leadership and clients a heavily manual process.
goals: Build standardised dashboards covering KPIs, SLA adherence, billing, and utilisation.
Automate the underlying data workflows to cut manual reporting effort.
Keep governance compliant through structured financial approval forms and scope trackers.
painPoints: Source data lived across four separate systems with no unified reporting layer.
Reporting was manual, which limited time available for actual analysis.
Quality breaches and delivery delays needed cross-team coordination to resolve.

approach.methodology: Built and maintained 50+ Power BI and Excel dashboards, automated SQL and Excel-based data workflows, and consolidated data from Replicon, Hive, Jira, and Adobe Workfront into structured reports for senior leadership and client-facing stakeholders. Also designed financial approval forms and scope trackers used as project contracts to keep governance compliant.

insights: Automating SQL and Excel-based workflows reduced manual reporting time by 70%.
Coordinating across Resource Management, Project Controllers, and Client Success teams resolved quality breaches and delivery delays more consistently.
recommendations: Keep the automated reporting workflow as the standard rather than reverting to manual consolidation.
Extend the same financial approval/scope-tracker governance model to new engagements as a default.
projects.roi: 70% reduction in manual reporting time, freeing team capacity for higher-value analysis across 7 networks and 80+ agencies.

projects.title: Recruitment Analytics Dashboard
projects.tagline: Uncovering hiring trends from 1,000+ survey responses to inform marketing and BD strategy.

businessProblem.context: Marketing and business development teams needed a clearer read on hiring trends and candidate engagement patterns than raw survey exports could provide.
goals: Analyse survey data from 1,000+ participants to surface hiring trends.
Build dashboards that make recruitment metrics usable for non-technical stakeholders.
Communicate findings clearly to a professional audience in real time.
painPoints: Survey data on its own didn't reveal actionable patterns without structured analysis.
Stakeholders needed insights presented live, not just as a static report.

approach.methodology: Analysed survey datasets from 1,000+ participants using SQL and Excel to uncover hiring trends and candidate engagement patterns, then built interactive dashboards to visualise the recruitment metrics.

insights: Interactive dashboards surfaced hiring trends and engagement patterns not visible in the raw survey exports.
Presenting findings live during LinkedIn virtual sessions made the insights more actionable for stakeholders in real time.
recommendations: Use the dashboard as a recurring input to marketing and business development planning, not a one-off analysis.
projects.roi: Enabled data-driven hiring-trend decisions for marketing and business development teams.
